Preface The best way to have control over something is to understand it. AI has disrupted several industries, and speculations of both its positive and negative effects flood the internet every day. It’s hard to know what is really a prediction versus merely a speculation from someone with a vested interest. In short, while AI has progressed rapidly, the general public has not been able to keep pace with the internals of how it works. And as a consequence, people have to rely on sensational opinions rather than observing the facts for themselves. This book changes that. It allows people to: 1. Understand the principles on which LLMs work, without all the math. 2. Learn both the advantages and limitations

facts for themselves. This book changes that. It allows people to: 1. Understand the principles on which LLMs work, without all the math. 2. Learn both the advantages and limitations of LLMs. 3. Know exactly what happens when an LLM runs, reasons, creates, so that they don’t mistake what’s happening for something else. 4. Protect themselves from speculations and false information about AI. And come to their own conclusions. 5. Dive deeper into the highly technical details of LLMs if they choose, using this book as a high-level map. 6. This book is for software engineers, product managers, content marketers, CEOs, CTOs, accountants, salespeople, and just about everyone else.

As of 2025, I lead AI for a major SaaS product, setting the vision, inventing frameworks, and packaging them into a wonderful product experience.

Preface ii I’ve been programming since the age of 11 (almost 25 years as of 2025). I’m an undergrad- uate in Electronics & Telecommunications Engineering, and have a Master’s in Computer Science from GeorgiaTech, specializing in Machine Learning. I love learning from first princi- ples rather than memorizing or speculating. Over the years, I’ve architected complex high-scale systems, led big teams, designed differentiators, and have seen how business & technology intertwine behind closed room meetings with prominent leaders. In writing this book, I’ve tried to combine the knowledge of AI, with my approach of thinking from first principles, and put them in the context of the business of technology so that what I cover is both clear and useful. Chapter 1 Cutting Through the AI Jargon “Define your terms, you will permit me again to say, or we shall never understand one another.” — Voltaire (translated) Terms around AI, like reasoning or thinking, are used loosely by the mainstream media and startups. This leads to heated debates around how conscious AI really is, or what kind of jobs it’s coming for next. Instead of choosing the route of debate, we’ll choose that of understanding. However, before we can talk about what AI can or cannot do, we must first agree on the definitions of words like intelligence, learning, and a few others. We’ll also focus solely on large language models (LLMs) rather than the much broader field of AI.

agree on the definitions of words like intelligence, learning, and a few others. We’ll also focus solely on large language models (LLMs) rather than the much broader field of AI. Fundamental Concepts Before jumping into LLMs, you’ll benefit from understanding some of the fundamental terms generally used within AI and its subfield of machine learning. These words are the key to why people often exaggerate the claims around AI or fail to understand its true impact. Learn Learn is the most misunderstood word in AI. It doesn’t quite mean what people often think. In computers, to learn means to find patterns, not to understand concepts.
people often think. In computers, to learn means to find patterns, not to understand concepts.

Cutting Through the AI Jargon 2 For example, when you read the word table, you might picture a table in your head. You instantly know how it’s used, what type of things you’d keep on it, and probably what kind of chair you’d want with it. In short, people read to understand and infer meaning. We don’t merely infer patterns. For us, patterns are only as useful as they are meaningful. An AI algorithm, however, primarily learns patterns. When it looks at words like dog or cat, it merely notices the typical words that surround them. That’s how it knows that dogs and cats are related, because these words appear in similar contexts. (Illustrated below) Dogs are very cute animals.

the typical words that surround them. That’s how it knows that dogs and cats are related, because these words appear in similar contexts. (Illustrated below) Dogs are very cute animals. So are cats. You just need to feed them well and take good care of them. AI is like using tricks to memorize a subject to pass a university exam, without any deep understanding of the ideas being taught. Thus, when you see the word learn used in the context of AI, you read it as pattern recognition rather than true understanding. This is precisely also the reason why LLMs need large amount of data to “understand” a single word, whereas people don’t. Artificial Intelligence Peter Norvig, one of AI’s

true understanding. This is precisely also the reason why LLMs need large amount of data to “understand” a single word, whereas people don’t. Artificial Intelligence Peter Norvig, one of AI’s leading thinkers, defines AI as any system that acts rationally in a specific environment. It’s important to realize that the emphasis is on: 1. Actions: We’re not talking about how AI achieves its action; just that it acts in a manner that we can call intelligent. 2. Environment: Keeping the debates about Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) aside, we confine actions to be in a specific environment. That same software might not act intelligently in a slightly different environment.
to be in a specific environment. That same software might not act intelligently in a slightly different environment.

Cutting Through the AI Jargon 3 It’s that definition why even simple games of the 80s had AI opponents, despite being primitive. Our definition sees the action of AI as being analogical to humans, rather than the internal thought processes. In short, if it floats like a duck, swims like a duck, and quacks like a duck, then it’s a duck in that particular environment—even if it’s not really a duck. For example, a spam filter that correctly separates junk mail from real messages is intelli- gent in the environment of an email inbox. Its world is narrow, and its actions are limited. But within that, it is as intelligent as it can correctly classify messages. Thus, the definition of

of an email inbox. Its world is narrow, and its actions are limited. But within that, it is as intelligent as it can correctly classify messages. Thus, the definition of intelligence for machines is not quite the same as that for humans. Our definition of machine intelligence is about acting rationally in a specific environment; not mirroring human thoughts exactly. Machine Learning (ML) Machine learning is the method by which systems learn patterns from data rather than being explicitly programmed. Thus, ML systems generalize patterns from data automati- cally. Machine learning is a sub-field of AI. While ML focuses on pattern-recognition, AI broadly focuses on making decisions and acting effectively given those patterns. Here’s a quick example of how ML

learning is a sub-field of AI. While ML focuses on pattern-recognition, AI broadly focuses on making decisions and acting effectively given those patterns. Here’s a quick example of how ML looks. Consider a table as follows: x (input) y (output) 1 4 2 8 3 12 An ML model takes all input-output pairs from the training data above and learns the rela- tionship between them. In this particular case, the relationship is y = 4x. That relationship is the trained model. Now, whenever you give it a new input that was not in the training data, it predicts the output. For example, for an input of 7, the model will output 28 (y = 4x = 4*7 = 28). Now,

was not in the training data, it predicts the output. For example, for an input of 7, the model will output 28 (y = 4x = 4*7 = 28). Now, this kind of learning might appear wasteful for simple equations. But for messy, real- world problems (like recognizing faces or detecting spam), the rules are too complex to write by hand. Machine learning finds those rules by generalizing from examples auto- matically. Remember: ML is about generalization, not memorization. And it generalizes by inferring patterns.

Cutting Through the AI Jargon 4 Machine Learning Models A model is the result of training. You can now give new inputs to the model to have it predict the corresponding outputs. Terms like linear regression, decision trees, or neural networks refer to specific ways of imple- menting machine learning. They are merely different ways of finding patterns in data. LLMs are also one such kind of model. Deep Learning Deep learning simply refers to training neural networks with many layers (hence deep) so that they can capture more abstract patterns. More layers allow model architectures that can capture more abstract patterns (shown below). But it also then needs more computing power and data. That’s why deep learning became practical

That’s why deep learning became practical only once GPUs were powerful enough to train models with millions or even billions of parameters. Lower-level features learnt by initial layers Which translates eventually to higher-level features as seen by the deeper layers LLM-Related Concepts Now that we’re clear on the foundational concepts, let’s dive into terms specifically used in the world of LLMs. These definitions are not meant to be technically precise, but work - able enough for our purposes.

Cutting Through the AI Jargon 5 Foundation Models A foundation model is trained on vast, general-purpose data so it can later be adapted to specific tasks like writing code, summarizing text, or generating SQL queries. Think of GPT-5 as the foundation; ChatGPT is the product experience built on top of it using specialized versions of such foundation models. It’s important to note that the term is foundation and not foundational. Stanford researchers introduced the term foundation model in 2021 to emphasize the idea of building upon a common base. T oken LLMs process tokens, which are chunks of text that may be whole words or just parts of words. For instance, “happiness” might split into “happi” + “ness.” Tokenization, which

oken LLMs process tokens, which are chunks of text that may be whole words or just parts of words. For instance, “happiness” might split into “happi” + “ness.” Tokenization, which is the process of converting input text into tokens, helps keep vocab- ulary size manageable and allows the model to handle rare or invented words gracefully. In this book, we will use the terms tokens and words interchangeably for simplicity. Auto-Regressive Models Auto-regressive models generate text one token at a time, feeding each newly generated token back as input to predict the next. This is why you see applications like ChatGPT appearing to type out their responses instead of generating the output text all at once. The quick brown The

predict the next. This is why you see applications like ChatGPT appearing to type out their responses instead of generating the output text all at once. The quick brown The quick brown fox The quick brown fox jumps CONTEXT NEW TOKEN 1] CONTEXT NEW TOKEN 2] 3] CONTEXT NEW TOKEN Transformers Transformers are a neural-network model architecture designed to process sequences, like text, using a mechanism called attention. Attention allows the models to learn rich representations, support longer contexts, and many other benefits of a technical nature. Attention refers to the fact that the model can weigh which prior tokens matter the most in predicting the next one.
the fact that the model can weigh which prior tokens matter the most in predicting the next one.

Cutting Through the AI Jargon 6 Large language models have many uses. They can write SQL and even Python. Note how when processing “They”, the attention mechanism realizes that it refers to “Large language models.” Diffusion Models Diffusion models are used primarily for generating images. They start from random noise and gradually refine it into a meaningful image. In this book, we will only talk about auto-regressive models and not diffusion models. GPT (Generative Pre- Trained Transformer) A trained auto-regressive model can be referred to as a GPT. It’s the architecture used by most modern language models. Generative means it can create new data (like text). Pre-trained means it has already absorbed broad language knowledge before fine-tuning for specific tasks.

Pre-trained means it has already absorbed broad language knowledge before fine-tuning for specific tasks. And Transformer is the architecture underneath that makes it all work. Summary 1. Learning in the context of AI is primarily about patterns and not concep- tual understanding. 2. AI is about acting intelligently within a specific environment. 3. Machine Learning is how systems generalize patterns from data. 4. Foundation Models are the general bases, trained on vast amounts of data, from which specialized AI systems are built. 5. The Transformer architecture powers modern LLMs. Tokens are the units of text that they operate on.

Chapter 2 A Crash Course on How LLMs Work “He who loves practice without theory is like the sailor who boards ship without a rudder and compass and never knows where he may cast.” – Leonardo da Vinci In this chapter, you’ll learn the basic principle on which LLMs operate; ones that allow them to answer questions, write emails, or even SQL code. Knowing this will clarify the strengths and limitations of LLMs instead of you having to rely on arbitrary opinions. The Scope LLMs have many technical aspects, like the model architecture and the intricacies of the training process. We won’t dive into those mathematical and technical details in our anal- ysis. Instead, we will focus on the basic

the model architecture and the intricacies of the training process. We won’t dive into those mathematical and technical details in our anal- ysis. Instead, we will focus on the basic principle on which LLMs operate. We’ll further see how this surprisingly naïve-looking principle manages to work magically in the real-world. Also, note that we’ll focus only on text-generation LLMs. When you use ChatGPT or Claude or Gemini and ask them questions, text is what you’re primarily dealing with. Image generation models, however, work on a different set of principles. Whatever you read is only applicable to LLMs for text generation and not necessarily image models. Finally, although we use English as the language in all our examples, most LLMs are

you read is only applicable to LLMs for text generation and not necessarily image models. Finally, although we use English as the language in all our examples, most LLMs are multi-lingual and support a variety of languages. However, the fundamental principle of operation remains the same.

A Crash Course on How LLMs Work 8 The Magical Basic Principle You learned about auto-regressive models in the first chapter. They primarily work by learning to predict the next word (or token, about which we’ll learn in the next chapter). And they do this one word at a time as follows: The quick brown The quick brown fox The quick brown fox jumps CONTEXT NEW TOKEN 1] CONTEXT NEW TOKEN 2] 3] CONTEXT NEW TOKEN Each time the model predicts a new word, the size of the context increases, and this increased context is then used to predict the next word, and so on. In short, the objective of the model is to complete texts (sentences, paragraphs, or anything

and this increased context is then used to predict the next word, and so on. In short, the objective of the model is to complete texts (sentences, paragraphs, or anything else) with syntactic and semantic coherence. That’s it. For example, imagine playing a game with several people, which plays out as follows: 1. You start with the phrase: “The world” 2. The next person chooses a word to follow it. Now the phrase becomes “The world is” 3. Now, another person chooses another word, and we get “The world is round” 4. The final person chooses a “.” (period). You’re left with the final sentence: “The world is round. ” Everything magical that LLMs do—be it writing emails, producing programs,

round” 4. The final person chooses a “.” (period). You’re left with the final sentence: “The world is round. ” Everything magical that LLMs do—be it writing emails, producing programs, or solving logical puzzles—relies on this basic principle. It’s hard to believe that merely predicting the next word could lead to the magic that we call AI, but that’s exactly what it is. Next, let’s look at how we use this principle to make LLMs do specific tasks.

A Crash Course on How LLMs Work 9 Setting up LLMs for Specific Tasks The next question is, how do we exploit this predict-the-next-word behavior to get LLMs to perform real-world tasks? Consider the task of writing emails. We can trick the LLM into drafting one, while it is still just predicting the next word. We do that by priming the LLM cleverly with context that has both the instructions and the beginning of the email. The LLM then auto-completes the rest of the email as follows: PREDICTED CONTEXT I’m writing an email announcing the launch of a new product. It would be professional, concise, yet hard-hitting. Here’s the email: Hello, Today I’m excited to announce the launch of our

writing an email announcing the launch of a new product. It would be professional, concise, yet hard-hitting. Here’s the email: Hello, Today I’m excited to announce the launch of our newest product — built to deliver faster performance, greater reliability, and a dramatically improved experience. This release represents months of re/f_inement, customer feedback, and rigorous testing. With it, you’ll gain: ... Here’s what we did: 1. We provided a context to begin with, which we then ask the LLM to complete. The context cleverly guides the LLM’s text generation. 2. The context doesn’t just start with an email, but some text that describes what we’re trying to achieve. This is a trick to provide instructions to the LLM on how

The context doesn’t just start with an email, but some text that describes what we’re trying to achieve. This is a trick to provide instructions to the LLM on how and what the email content should be like. 3. Finally, we also start some bits of the email. This is not strictly necessary, but it ensures that whatever the LLM generates next would be the rest of the email. And that email will conform to whatever instructions we started with. 4. Given the context above, the LLM then keeps generating words. And hope- fully, we’d have a coherent piece of text that represents an email. Thus, the context molds the LLM’s predict-the-next-word behavior and helps us get LLMs to perform

words. And hope- fully, we’d have a coherent piece of text that represents an email. Thus, the context molds the LLM’s predict-the-next-word behavior and helps us get LLMs to perform specific tasks. This is despite the underlying operating principle being simple (and often naïve).

A Crash Course on How LLMs Work 10 Setting up a Coding Task Let’s also look at setting up an LLM to do a coding task. This will reinforce the importance of the context in getting LLMs to do the right thing. In most blog posts online, explanations are usually followed by code examples. Thus the LLM has seen the pattern of text followed with code before. We leverage this to cleverly craft a context as follows that we then ask the LLM to auto-complete: CONTEXT PREDICTED The following code implements bubble sort in Python in a very simple fashion. Here it is: Note that the LLM follows it with some text and then code (which is a representative example).

implements bubble sort in Python in a very simple fashion. Here it is: Note that the LLM follows it with some text and then code (which is a representative example). It might also add some explanations after the code, as is often seen in real-world usage. You can now start seeing a pattern here. The problems that LLMs primarily solve are set up such that a predict-the-next-word approach works to solve it. How LLMs Know When to Stop If LLMs keep predicting the next word, they’ll never stop. How is it that after a point, the LLM decides that it has answered the question, or has finished writing the code? There is a special indicator (think of it as a

it that after a point, the LLM decides that it has answered the question, or has finished writing the code? There is a special indicator (think of it as a special word) called the End of Sequence (EOS) indicator. When the LLM itself predicts EOS as the next word, we stop generating new words from the LLM and assume that whatever has been written so far is complete. (There can be multiple such special tokens used by LLMs.) For example, to respond with just one single sentence, the LLM would have to output: “The world is round.<EOS>” . As the end-users, we never see the EOS, but just everything before it.
have to output: “The world is round.<EOS>” . As the end-users, we never see the EOS, but just everything before it.

A Crash Course on How LLMs Work 11 LLMs in the Real World When using real-world LLMs like ChatGPT, you do not give them texts to auto-complete. Instead, you ask questions and receive precise answers. This happens because such LLMs are further instruction-tuned, which means further trained on specialized datasets where both the questions and answers are provided to the LLM. Let’s first consider what would happen without instruction tuning: the LLM might just complete the question itself with more details, instead of actually furnishing an answer. And that’s not what we want. However, if we do instruction-tune the LLM, then the LLM learns that questions or requests are often followed by answers, and thus learns to respond to questions.

what we want. However, if we do instruction-tune the LLM, then the LLM learns that questions or requests are often followed by answers, and thus learns to respond to questions. It is still predicting the next words, but the pattern of that prediction changes. This is not very important to dive deeper into at this time. You just have to understand that the predict-the-next-word concept is still very much in action. And just because an LLM gives you smarter answers with each new version doesn’t necessarily change the basic principle on which it operates. Why Context is Important With whatever we’ve learned so far, you’d now appreciate why contexts are so important with LLMs. Both the length of the context,

on which it operates. Why Context is Important With whatever we’ve learned so far, you’d now appreciate why contexts are so important with LLMs. Both the length of the context, and the ability of the LLM to use that context effectively are important, because longer context means we can get more nuanced and sophisticated answers. The formulation of any problem depends on how well we engineer the context. That context is what we also call the prompt (from which prompt engineering comes). Getting things done using an LLM is not always as simple as writing to it as you would to a friend. One has to understand how responses are generated, and then leverage the nuances of that process, to

always as simple as writing to it as you would to a friend. One has to understand how responses are generated, and then leverage the nuances of that process, to then get the right responses from the LLM. System Prompts & Conversations The system prompt, user prompt, and even the entire conversation are all context for the LLM. When the LLM responds to a long conversation, it’s using the entire conversation history as the context from which to generate the response. Thus, a conversation is just a specially formatted context, and not an entirely different type of input.
and not an entirely different type of input.

A Crash Course on How LLMs Work 12 The T ext is Not Meaning You have to always remember that LLMs only operate in the text domain, and don’t primarily work on the meaning of texts like humans. Instead, they simulate meaning by learning patterns around text. Like we talked about in Chapter 1, the LLMs don’t really get the concept of the words encountered, but rather only rely on other surrounding words to predict the next word. It’s we who assign meaning to things: both inputs and outputs. When we write a prompt, all that the model sees is text, but we have taken some meaning and represented it as text. When the model outputs text, it is we

we write a prompt, all that the model sees is text, but we have taken some meaning and represented it as text. When the model outputs text, it is we who then interpret it and then apply it to the real world. The analogy is much like a calculator. You can input numbers and get numbers back. But that doesn’t make the calculator a mathematician. It is we who interpret and give mean- ings to numbers. A 6 is just a 6 to a calculator regardless of whether we’re talking about the quantity of apples, or someone’s height, or the time of the day. It’s just that the LLM is a higher dimensional calculator, which can often give an illusion

about the quantity of apples, or someone’s height, or the time of the day. It’s just that the LLM is a higher dimensional calculator, which can often give an illusion that it’s more than a calculator. But note that all LLMs run on standard computers of today, which at their core have the “Arithmetic and Logic Unit,” whose operations are just prim- itive arithmetic operations like add/subtract, and logical operations like AND/OR. Every - thing you see the LLM do is broken down into these operations, even if it looks surprising on how adding and multiplying numbers could ever produce coherent texts. But it does! The takeaway is that we assign meaning. No meaning is inherent to an LLM; only

surprising on how adding and multiplying numbers could ever produce coherent texts. But it does! The takeaway is that we assign meaning. No meaning is inherent to an LLM; only symbols are inherent to LLMs. Summary 1. The basic principle of operation of LLMs is predicting the next word. By doing this repeatedly, entire responses are generated. AI is about acting intel- ligently within a specific environment. 2. By cleverly using this basic principle, we can get the LLM to perform intel- ligently on many tasks in specific environments. 3. Despite that, LLMs only operate on texts and not their meanings. It’s we who assign meanings to both inputs and outputs.
Despite that, LLMs only operate on texts and not their meanings. It’s we who assign meanings to both inputs and outputs.

Chapter 3 The Word is a Lie “Details make perfection, and perfection is not a detail.” – Leonardo da Vinci We’ve covered the basic principle of how LLMs work in the previous chapter. However, there are two refinements that we can make to our understanding. The first is in knowing how words are seen by an LLM. We talked about how LLMs don’t really understand the meaning of words but only see them in relation to other surrounding words. We’ll understand just how they do this using some light math. The second is in looking at words themselves more closely. Actually, instead of words, LLMs operate on tokens. In practice, these two are usually interchangeable, and don’t really change how

The second is in looking at words themselves more closely. Actually, instead of words, LLMs operate on tokens. In practice, these two are usually interchangeable, and don’t really change how you might use LLMs. But we cover tokens because the term is widely used. Let’s begin. How LLMs See Words We’ll use some light math to briefly see how LLMs model relationships between words without really understanding the meaning of any particular word. LLMs represent each word as a list of numbers, also called embeddings, as shown in the figure below: running: [0.67, 0.10, -0.29, 0.58, 0.21] ocean: [0.55, -0.02, 0.76, -0.40, 0.18] Words and their example embeddings (list of numbers)

The Word is a Lie 14 Now, note the following about these embeddings: 1. The list of numbers associated with each word is arbitrary. It does not mean anything specifically by itself. These numbers are derived during the training process. 2. However, these numbers allow us to mathematically model the relation- ship between words. These numbers are such that they can relate to numbers of other words. For example, it’s through these numbers that we say that the words like dogs and cats are probably related, and to what extent are they similar. 3. This mechanism has a surprising implication. Hypothetically, if the words “hello” and “world” were used in exactly the same way in the LLM’s training data, the

are they similar. 3. This mechanism has a surprising implication. Hypothetically, if the words “hello” and “world” were used in exactly the same way in the LLM’s training data, the LLM would see no difference between those words and behave as if they were the same. The numbers make it obvious that no meaning is being encoded behind these words, the way humans would. Instead, the numbers allow a trained LLM to encode patterns of grammar, logic, and anything else. For example, think of the numbers 5 & 3, and 12 & 10. Although the numbers are themselves different, their difference is 2 in both cases, meaning that their relationships are the same. This brings us back to the analogy

12 & 10. Although the numbers are themselves different, their difference is 2 in both cases, meaning that their relationships are the same. This brings us back to the analogy from Chapter 1. How LLMs are Trained 25 Post the training process, this is what is learned: 1. The embeddings (list of numbers) against each token. Again, these numbers are arbitrary by themselves, but maintain a specific relationship with the numbers of other tokens. Embeddings encode the static relationships between tokens. For example, we can infer the general static relationship that “dogs” and “cats” are similar by looking at their embeddings alone. 2. The values of all the neural network parameters which now helps the model predict next tokens given the current context. The LLM learns patterns of grammar, logical statements, and any other dynamic patterns of relation- ships that can be inferred from text. For example, in the sentence, “The dog chased the cat,” who’s chasing whom is a dynamic relationship. LLMs use billions of parameters to learn these patterns effectively, which is another factor (apart from the training data size) that contributes to long training time and heavy resource usage. How LLMs are Trained 26 An LLM would associate “they” with “large language models” in the above piece of text. The Next Steps Once you’ve trained such a model, you have what we’d call a foundation model. This is the model that can auto-complete whatever texts you pass to it. However, to make such models answer questions or follow instructions, we need to instruction-tune this founda- tion model further. That’s what we’ll look at next.

How LLMs are Trained 27 Instruction- Tuning the Model In Chapter 2, we talked about how as users, we don’t ask LLMs to auto-complete our texts. Rather, we give it instructions and questions and expect an appropriate response. We achieve this by taking a foundation model, and then further fine-tuning it on special- ized datasets spanning many specific tasks. That’s how the model learns how to predict next tokens in a manner that allows it to write SQL, code, emails, poems, and the likes. This kind of fine-tuning to make LLMs follow instructions is also called instruction-tuning. What’s Fine- Tuning? Fine-tuning is a fancy name, but it still means training. We take a foundation model and further train it on some more specialized data. The training data for fine-tuning are generally smaller in volume, and we also only train the model for a few cycles. This way, the model learns the specialized task, but doesn’t forget its original training on the vast dataset. Why LLMs Don’t Give the Same Answer Twice 40 It’s important to note that a different token choice very early in the text can lead to an entirely different subsequent text. In the above case, the options are very symmetrical, but that needn’t be the case in the real world. Each time you make a different choice, you diverge to that degree. And by the time you’re at the last token, you’ve diverged quite a bit. On Meaning Again We’ve repeated quite a few times that LLMs don’t see meaning, but only surrounding words. And this whole concept of decoding further reinforces that idea. An LLM can generate something drastically different, just because it took a different turn while gener- ating a response to the same prompt. However, those different turns need not always just be stylistic changes! In other words, the answers of LLMs can also vary in meaning and not just wording, for the exact same prompt. The confusion of opinions often masquerading as facts often invades the training data of the LLM, which only sees text and doesn’t differentiate between opinions and facts. The only difference being, instead of having a question and answer while fine-tuning, we have a question and an answer that’s divided into reasoning and final answer texts. The reasoning sections are usually hidden by default when we use LLMs This is curated specially to teach LLMs to follow certain patterns of reasoning when addressing questions. This allows LLMs to synthesize complex new facts from previous ones. Those new facts can be the answer the user’s expecting. Or those new facts can be used to help do something creative: like finding the implications of wanting a “luxury feel” before beginning to make the design.

How Reasoning Works in LLMs 71 Summary 1. Reasoning is the intermediate text generated by the LLM before it gener- ates the answer. This allows the LLM to synthesize new logical facts. 2. Reasoning is akin to the LLM itself generating context rather than being explicitly provided. 3. During training, the LLMs are provided with this additional reasoning data on specialized reasoning datasets. 4. Despite all of the above, reasoning is still achieved using text generation patterns rather than anything else.
